sections.paragraph:
ἡσυχάζω fut. ἡσυχάσω LXX; 1 aor. ἡσύχασα, impv. ἡσύχασον (s. three next entries; Solon, Fgm. 4c, 1 West=4, 5 Diehl; Aeschyl.,Thu. et al.; pap, LXX, Philo, Joseph., Test12Patr) in our lit. only intrans.

① to relax from normal activity, rest (Appian, Bell. Civ. 4, 72 §306; JosAs 10:8 ἐν τῇ κλίνῃ μοὺ; Jos., Ant. 18, 354) 1 Cl 4:5 (Gen 4:7); abstain fr. work (Herodian 7, 5, 3) of the conduct prescribed in the law for the sabbath Lk 23:56 (Neptunianus [II a.d.] ed. WGemoll, Progr. Striegau 1884, 53 the ants are said τὸ σάββατον ἡσυχάζειν κ. σχολὴν ἄγειν).

② to live a quiet life or refrain from disturbing activity, be peaceable/orderly (Thu. 1, 120, 3 w. εἰρήνη as synonym and opp. πολεμεῖν; BGU 372 II, 14; PSI 41, 23 σωφρονῖν καὶ ἡσυχάζειν; Philo, Abr. 27). Of conduct that does not disturb the peace. Christian leaders endeavored to keep their members free of anything that might be construed as disturbance of public order: 1 Th 4:11. In a semantically dense statement: w. gen. (for the gen. in connection with verbs expressing cessation, s. Kühner-Gerth II/1, 396) cease from (cp. Job 32:6) τῆς ματαίας στάσεως (here στάσις ‘disorder’ is opp. of ἡσυχία ‘order’, in ref. to corporate congregational life) 1 Cl 63:1.

③ to be free from being disturbed, have rest (Diog. L. 3, 21) ἀπό τινος from someth., remain undisturbed 1 Cl 57:7 (Pr 1:33), unless the ἀπό-clause goes w. ἀφόβως, in which case this pass. belongs under mng. 1. False v.l. cod. a ἡσύχαζον for ἀπηλαύνετο GJs 18:3.

④ to refrain from saying someth., be quiet, remain silent (Aeschyl., Prom. 327; ViAesopi G 38; Job 32:1; 2 Esdr 15:8; Philo, Somn. 2, 263; Jos., Bell. 3, 263, Ant. 1, 339) Lk 14:4; Ac 11:18; 21:14; 22:2 D (for ἡσυχία).—DELG s.v. ἥσυχος. M-M. TW. Spicq.

fut. fut. = fut.

LXX LXX = Septuaginta, ed. ARahlfs, unless otherwise specified—Lists 2, beg.

aor. aor. = aorist

impv. impv. = imperative

Solon Solon , VII–VI b.c.—List 5

Fgm. Fgm. = fragment, fragmentary

West West = MWest, Iambi et Elegi Graeci—List 5

Diehl Diehl , ed., s. AnthLG—List 5

Aeschyl Aeschyl , V b.c.—List 5

Thu Thu , V b.c.—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

pap pap = papyrus, -yri

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Joseph Joseph This abbr. used when no specific texts are cited (s. Jos.); I a.d.—List 5

Test12Patr Test12Patr = Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs, with interpolations II b.c.–III a.d.—List 2

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

Appian Appian , I–II a.d.—List 5

JosAs JosAs = Joseph and Aseneth—Lists 2, 5

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

1 Cl 1 Cl = 1 Clement—List 1

  1. fr. = from

Herodian Herodian = Herodianus Historicus, III a.d.—List 5

  1. ed. = edited (by), edition

opp. opp. = opposed to, opposite

BGU BGU = Aegyptische Urkunden aus den Museen zu Berlin: Griechische Urkunden—List 4

PSI PSI = Papiri greci e latini: Pubblicazioni della Società Italiana—List 4

gen. gen. = genitive

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

ref. ref. = reference(s)

Diog. L. Diog. L. = Diogenes Laertius, III a.d.—List 5

someth. someth. = something

pass. pass. = passive (either of grammatical form or of passive experience); also used in reference to literary portion=passage

mng. mng. = meaning(s)

v.l. v.l. = varia lectio (variant reading)

GJs GJs = Gospel of James (Protevangelium Jacobi), II a.d.—List 1

D D = Didache, except that in a list of manuscripts or as textual variant D refers to Codex Bezae—List 1

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

TW TW = Theologisches Wörterbuch zum NT; tr. GBromiley, Theological Dictionary of the NT—List 6

Spicq Spicq = CS., Lexique théologique du Nouveau Testament—Lists 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 440
